data.title: The Cargo Run Gone Sideways
data.premise: You've picked up a seemingly simple cargo job on Ariel—transport a crate of medical supplies to the border moon of Ita. But the cargo's more valuable than advertised, and now you've got Alliance inspectors, a desperate black-market buyer, and your own crew's conflicting interests all bearing down on Serenity. The clock is ticking, and every choice could mean profit, prison, or worse.
data.central_question: Can you navigate the competing factions and keep your crew safe while making the score that'll keep Serenity flying?

setting.name: The Ariel-Ita Shipping Corridor
setting.description: You're docked at Ariel's secondary port, a maze of legal gray zones where legitimate traders and black-market runners cross paths daily. Your ship, Serenity, sits ready to fly. Ita, your destination, is a lawless moon in Alliance-controlled space—perfect for deals that don't bear scrutiny. The route between them is heavily patrolled but full of hiding spots for those who know where to look.
setting.tone_notes: Lighthearted but tense. The danger is real, but so are the comedic fumbles, witty banter, and scrappy improvisation that define the Firefly universe. There's humor in the chaos—crew members bickering, elaborate plans falling apart, and Mal's stubborn determination to do the right thing while still making a profit. The mood swings between scheming, camaraderie, and narrow escapes, all grounded in the show's signature blend of space-western charm and found-family warmth.

acts.title: The Job Looks Easy
acts.description: You meet your contact at a dockside bar and agree to transport a crate of 'medical supplies' to Ita for a tidy sum. Your crew helps load the cargo with minimal questions. But as you're prepping for departure, Wash intercepts an Alliance patrol dispatch—they're looking for something matching your cargo's description. You realize the job might be hotter than advertised, and you've got to decide whether to abort, proceed with caution, or dig deeper into what you're really carrying.
acts.end_condition: Player makes a decision about whether to proceed with the job despite Alliance interest, or attempts to investigate the cargo
locked_constraints: The cargo must not be opened or inspected in detail yet
No direct confrontation with Alliance forces
The true nature of the cargo is not yet fully revealed
key_beats: Meeting the contact and accepting the job
Loading the mysterious crate aboard Serenity
Wash's warning about the Alliance patrol
Mal's internal conflict about profit vs. risk
Crew discussion about the suspicious cargo

acts.title: Everything Falls Apart
acts.description: Whether you investigate or proceed, complications multiply. An Alliance boarding party intercepts you in open space, forcing an evasive maneuver that damages Serenity's port engine. You escape, but now you're running on fumes—literally and figuratively. Your contact from Ariel goes silent. A second buyer emerges on the black-market frequency, offering triple the original price but demanding the cargo at a dangerous waypoint. Your crew is split: some want to ditch the cargo and limp home, others see the profit opportunity, and at least one has a secret stake in what's inside the crate.
acts.end_condition: Player chooses to either accept the second buyer's offer, attempt to reach original contact, or take a third option that puts crew at odds
locked_constraints: Serenity must sustain engine damage that limits options
The original contact cannot be easily reached
At least one crew member must have conflicting loyalties
key_beats: Contact goes dark; no communication possible
New buyer appears on black-market frequency
Crew disagreement about the job's viability
Revelation of a crew member's hidden connection to the cargo
Mal forced to choose between profit and crew safety

acts.title: Making the Run
acts.description: Mal commits to a path—deliver to the new buyer, salvage the original deal, or something riskier entirely. The final act is a tense race to the destination, with Alliance forces closing in, the new buyer's mercenaries waiting at the rendezvous, and Serenity limping along on one good engine. Mal must use cunning, crew loyalty, and a bit of luck to outrun both the law and the criminal underworld. The resolution depends on choices made: some paths lead to a clean escape with profit, others to a narrow getaway with consequences, and the moral weight of what was really in that crate becomes impossible to ignore.
acts.end_condition: Player reaches the designated endpoint, evades or defeats pursuing forces, and resolves the cargo situation
locked_constraints: The Alliance must remain a persistent threat
Serenity's engine damage must factor into the escape plan
The true contents of the cargo must be revealed

Serenity makes the run with pursuing forces
The cargo's true nature is revealed
Confrontation with either the Alliance or the new buyer
Clever use of Serenity's unique capabilities or crew talents to escape
Resolution: profit, loss, or moral victory

factions.description: The authoritarian government seeking to recover black-market cargo that threatens their control

factions.name: Independent Crime Syndicate
factions.description: Black-market operatives and smugglers competing for valuable goods
factions.goals: Acquire the cargo and eliminate rival buyers; maintain underworld dominance

world_rules: The Alliance controls major shipping lanes and regularly conducts patrols, but enforcement is inconsistent at the border moons
Black-market deals are common but dangerous; betrayal is always a possibility
Serenity is fast but fragile; damage accumulates and affects escape options
Crew loyalty is Mal's greatest asset, but conflicting interests can fracture the group
Information is currency; what people know about the cargo determines their leverage
Moral choices have real consequences—helping innocents may cost profit, but betraying the crew costs everything

data.passage:
The Ariel docks smell like rust, recycled air, and desperation—the kind of place where fortunes change hands daily and nobody asks questions they don't want answered. You stand in the shadow of Serenity's cargo bay, arms crossed, watching the crane operator maneuver a crate the size of a man's coffin onto your ship's lift. It's marked "Medical Supplies—Fragile," which probably means it contains exactly none of those things.

Marcus Webb leans against a rusted shipping container nearby, casual as you've ever seen him. The fixer's got that easy smile he always wears when a job's about to make someone rich—or dead. "Straight run to Ita," he says, sliding a datapad across to you. "Forty-eight hours, forty thousand credits. Nobody asks what's in the box."

Your crew's scattered around the bay—Zoe double-checking the manifest, Kaylee cooing over Serenity's thrumming engines, Wash running pre-flight checks from the bridge. The others are below deck or in quarters. This is the kind of job that's supposed to be simple. Haul cargo, deliver cargo, get paid. Routine.

Then Wash's voice crackles through the comm: "Cap, we've got an Alliance patrol dispatch just came through on the cortex. They're looking for a shipping container matching this one's dimensions. Flagged it as 'contraband of interest.' You seeing this?"

Your stomach tightens. The crate's being sealed now, locked down tight. Whatever's in there, someone official wants it bad enough to patrol this sector.

A.text: Tell Webb the deal's off. Have the crew unload the crate immediately and cut ties before the Alliance shows up on your doorstep.

B.text: Proceed with the job as planned. The Alliance patrols these routes constantly—it's probably nothing. Get Serenity prepped for departure and leave before they catch up.

C.text: Call Webb aside and demand he tell you exactly what's in the crate and why the Alliance is interested. If he won't level with you, the job's dead.

D.text: Accept the job but tell the crew to prepare for trouble. Have Wash monitor Alliance frequencies, get Zoe to plot an alternate route off Ariel, and keep your hand near your pistol.

PASSAGE:
The Ariel docks smell like rust, recycled air, and desperation—the kind of place where fortunes change hands daily and nobody asks questions they don't want answered. You stand in the shadow of Serenity's cargo bay, arms crossed, watching the crane operator maneuver a crate the size of a man's coffin onto your ship's lift. It's marked "Medical Supplies—Fragile," which probably means it contains exactly none of those things.

Marcus Webb leans against a rusted shipping container nearby, casual as you've ever seen him. The fixer's got that easy smile he always wears when a job's about to make someone rich—or dead. "Straight run to Ita," he says, sliding a datapad across to you. "Forty-eight hours, forty thousand credits. Nobody asks what's in the box."

Your crew's scattered around the bay—Zoe double-checking the manifest, Kaylee cooing over Serenity's thrumming engines, Wash running pre-flight checks from the bridge. The others are below deck or in quarters. This is the kind of job that's supposed to be simple. Haul cargo, deliver cargo, get paid. Routine.

Then Wash's voice crackles through the comm: "Cap, we've got an Alliance patrol dispatch just came through on the cortex. They're looking for a shipping container matching this one's dimensions. Flagged it as 'contraband of interest.' You seeing this?"

Your stomach tightens. The crate's being sealed now, locked down tight. Whatever's in there, someone official wants it bad enough to patrol this sector.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Tell Webb the deal's off. Have the crew unload the crate immediately and cut ties before the Alliance shows up on your doorstep.
B: Proceed with the job as planned. The Alliance patrols these routes constantly—it's probably nothing. Get Serenity prepped for departure and leave before they catch up.
C: Call Webb aside and demand he tell you exactly what's in the crate and why the Alliance is interested. If he won't level with you, the job's dead.
D: Accept the job but tell the crew to prepare for trouble. Have Wash monitor Alliance frequencies, get Zoe to plot an alternate route off Ariel, and keep your hand near your pistol.

state_updates.event_log_entry: Mal arrives at Ariel docks to meet Marcus Webb for cargo job. Webb offers 40,000 credits to haul 'medical supplies' to Ita in 48 hours. Crate being loaded onto Serenity when Wash intercepts Alliance patrol dispatch flagging container as contraband.

A.consequence: Mal aborts the job cleanly. Webb accepts the cancellation without incident (he's a professional fixer). Crate is unloaded. Serenity remains docked but safe. Relationship with Webb drops slightly (-10) for wasting his time, but no permanent damage. Alliance patrol passes without incident. Crew remains unified. No profit, no risk.
A.narration_directive: Write Webb's reaction as professional disappointment—he shrugs, pockets the datapad, and moves on to find another hauler. Show the crew's relief mixed with frustration at lost credits. Emphasize the safety of the choice but the sting of missed opportunity. Keep tone light—this is the 'boring but smart' option.

B.consequence: Mal ignores the warning and commits to the run. Serenity launches on schedule. Within 2 hours of departure, Alliance patrol intercepts Serenity in open space. Wash executes evasive maneuvers, but the patrol is aggressive. During the chase, Serenity's port engine takes damage—not catastrophic, but enough to reduce speed and maneuverability. Escape is successful, but the ship is now limping. World flag 'serenity_engine_damaged' becomes true. Relationship with Wash increases (+5) for his piloting skill, but tension rises among crew as the danger becomes real. This choice locks Mal into Act 2 complications.
B.narration_directive: Write the chase sequence with tension and humor—Wash's quips, the ship shuddering, alarms blaring. Show the engine damage as a grinding, smoking problem that Kaylee will have to address. End with Serenity limping away from the patrol but clearly wounded. The Alliance knows Serenity is carrying the cargo now. Make it clear this was a gamble that partially paid off but created new problems.

C.consequence: Mal confronts Webb privately. Webb hesitates—he's clearly been told not to discuss contents—but respects Mal enough to give a partial truth: 'It's pharmaceutical. Black-market grade. High-value buyers on Ita. That's all I can say without getting people killed.' He won't elaborate further, but this confirms the cargo is hot. Webb's relationship stays neutral (+0 delta, but trust is reinforced). Mal gains critical information without triggering Alliance attention yet. The crate remains loaded. This choice sets up better decision-making in Act 2 because Mal now knows the cargo is pharmaceutical-grade contraband. Crew remains unified for now.
C.narration_directive: Write Webb as conflicted but honest—show him glancing around, lowering his voice, and giving Mal just enough truth to be useful without being suicidal. Emphasize that Webb respects Mal's directness. The tone should be tense but collegial. Mal should feel like he's gotten an advantage without tipping his hand to the Alliance.

D.consequence: Mal accepts the job and goes into full operational security mode. Crew mobilizes: Wash locks onto Alliance frequencies and begins monitoring, Zoe plots three alternate jump routes off Ariel, Mal keeps hand near pistol. Serenity launches on schedule with heightened readiness. Within 90 minutes of departure, Alliance patrol attempts to intercept, but Wash's advance warning and Zoe's alternate route allow Serenity to slip away cleanly—no engine damage, no direct confrontation. However, the Alliance now has Serenity's flight signature and knows the ship is running. Relationships with Wash (+10) and Zoe (+10) increase due to their competent execution. Mal's decisiveness strengthens crew unity (+5 overall). World flag 'escape_route_planned' becomes true. This choice demonstrates Mal's leadership and sets up Act 2 with the crew confident but the Alliance actively hunting.
D.narration_directive: Write this as a tense but successful operation—show Wash's focus, Zoe's tactical precision, and Mal's calm command presence. The escape should feel earned, not lucky. Emphasize that the crew worked together flawlessly and that Mal's preparation paid off. End with Serenity in open space, engines humming, but with the knowledge that the Alliance is now actively pursuing them. The tone should be 'we pulled it off, but they're coming.'

data.passage:
You catch Webb's arm as he turns to leave the cargo bay, steering him toward the shadow of a supply stack where prying eyes can't track the conversation. His jaw tightens—he knows what this means. You've got questions, and you're not the type to ask politely.

"Level with me," you say quietly. "What's really in the crate, and why does the Alliance give enough of a damn to patrol for it?"

Webb glances around, his weathered face creasing. For a moment you think he'll dodge you, feed you the same "medical supplies" line. But then he exhales, and something shifts in his eyes—respect, maybe, for a captain who won't fly blind.

"Pharmaceutical stock," he says, keeping his voice low. "Experimental antivirals. The kind the Alliance keeps locked down tight because they're worried about black-market distribution." He pauses, choosing his next words carefully. "The buyer on Ita—legitimate operation, mostly. But the Alliance got wind of the shipment and they're not keen on letting it slip through."

"That's it?" you ask.

"That's enough," Webb says. "The patrol you caught wind of—they're fishing. They don't know it's on your ship. Yet. But if you dock anywhere official or file a flight plan they can track, they'll narrow it down fast."

He pulls his arm free, meeting your eyes. "I respect that you asked, Mal. Means you're smart. Means you'll get it there."

A.text: Ask Webb if the Alliance is likely to escalate patrols, or if this is just routine screening. You need to know the heat level before you undock.

B.text: Tell Webb you're proceeding to Ita as planned, but you want his assurance the buyer is solid. If this deal goes sideways, he's answering for it.

C.text: Press Webb on whether he's got any contacts in the Alliance who might tip him off about increased patrols. Information like that could save your life.

D.text: Accept Webb's explanation and head back to Serenity to brief your crew. But first, ask if there's anything else he hasn't told you that could bite you in the black.

PASSAGE:
You catch Webb's arm as he turns to leave the cargo bay, steering him toward the shadow of a supply stack where prying eyes can't track the conversation. His jaw tightens—he knows what this means. You've got questions, and you're not the type to ask politely.

"Level with me," you say quietly. "What's really in the crate, and why does the Alliance give enough of a damn to patrol for it?"

Webb glances around, his weathered face creasing. For a moment you think he'll dodge you, feed you the same "medical supplies" line. But then he exhales, and something shifts in his eyes—respect, maybe, for a captain who won't fly blind.

"Pharmaceutical stock," he says, keeping his voice low. "Experimental antivirals. The kind the Alliance keeps locked down tight because they're worried about black-market distribution." He pauses, choosing his next words carefully. "The buyer on Ita—legitimate operation, mostly. But the Alliance got wind of the shipment and they're not keen on letting it slip through."

"That's it?" you ask.

"That's enough," Webb says. "The patrol you caught wind of—they're fishing. They don't know it's on your ship. Yet. But if you dock anywhere official or file a flight plan they can track, they'll narrow it down fast."

He pulls his arm free, meeting your eyes. "I respect that you asked, Mal. Means you're smart. Means you'll get it there."

A: Ask Webb if the Alliance is likely to escalate patrols, or if this is just routine screening. You need to know the heat level before you undock.
B: Tell Webb you're proceeding to Ita as planned, but you want his assurance the buyer is solid. If this deal goes sideways, he's answering for it.
C: Press Webb on whether he's got any contacts in the Alliance who might tip him off about increased patrols. Information like that could save your life.
D: Accept Webb's explanation and head back to Serenity to brief your crew. But first, ask if there's anything else he hasn't told you that could bite you in the black.

data.passage:
Webb glances over his shoulder, then leans in closer, keeping his voice low enough that the ambient noise of the cargo bay swallows his words. "Routine screening's what they want you to think," he says, jaw tight. "But their dispatch pattern? That's escalation. They're cross-referencing manifests against black-market intel—probably got a tip from someone on the inside. You've got maybe half a day before they start flagging every ship that docked here in the last seventy-two hours. After that, they'll want to inspect."

You feel the weight of it settle in your chest. Half a day. Not much room for error.

"The real danger," Webb continues, "is staying put. Sitting in port looks innocent until it doesn't. You undock, you're just another freighter heading out-system. You stay, you become suspicious. They'll use that as probable cause."

He straightens, and his expression hardens into something professional and final. "I've done my part. The buyer on Ita knows when to expect you. Move fast, keep your transponder quiet where you can, and don't make any runs that look panicked. That's how you get intercepted."

Behind you, you hear Kaylee's wrench clinking against something metallic—she's still working on Serenity's systems. Somewhere in the corridor, Jayne's boots echo. Your crew doesn't know what's in the crate yet, and they don't know the clock is ticking louder than it was five minutes ago.

A.text: Thank Webb and head straight to the bridge to brief Wash and Zoe on the timeline. You need your crew informed and prepped for immediate departure—no surprises when the engines fire up.

B.text: Ask Webb one more critical question: who's the buyer on Ita, and how solid is that connection? If the Alliance is hunting this cargo this hard, you need to know if the endpoint is actually safe.

C.text: Tell Webb to clear out and disappear. Then gather the crew in the galley for a full briefing—they deserve to know what they're flying with. Transparency now beats betrayal later.

D.text: Have Webb help you inspect the crate's seals and packaging before he goes. If the Alliance boards, you need to know exactly how obvious this cargo is, and whether it'll pass a cursory look.

- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock.

BRUTAL MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- The world is hostile. Consequences are immediate and devastating. When a choice goes wrong, it goes VERY wrong.
- Consequences compound and do not resolve on their own. The danger comes from the world being unforgiving, not from artificially limiting safe options — let the narrative situation dictate how many choices are risky on any given turn.
- TRAP LOGIC (Brutal-exclusive): Some choices that APPEAR safe MUST actually carry hidden severe consequences. Base these on earlier context the player may have missed — an NPC's hidden motive, a world flag they ignored, a warning sign in a previous passage. PASSAGE:
Webb glances over his shoulder, then leans in closer, keeping his voice low enough that the ambient noise of the cargo bay swallows his words. "Routine screening's what they want you to think," he says, jaw tight. "But their dispatch pattern? That's escalation. They're cross-referencing manifests against black-market intel—probably got a tip from someone on the inside. You've got maybe half a day before they start flagging every ship that docked here in the last seventy-two hours. After that, they'll want to inspect."

You feel the weight of it settle in your chest. Half a day. Not much room for error.

"The real danger," Webb continues, "is staying put. Sitting in port looks innocent until it doesn't. You undock, you're just another freighter heading out-system. You stay, you become suspicious. They'll use that as probable cause."

He straightens, and his expression hardens into something professional and final. "I've done my part. The buyer on Ita knows when to expect you. Move fast, keep your transponder quiet where you can, and don't make any runs that look panicked. That's how you get intercepted."

Behind you, you hear Kaylee's wrench clinking against something metallic—she's still working on Serenity's systems. Somewhere in the corridor, Jayne's boots echo. Your crew doesn't know what's in the crate yet, and they don't know the clock is ticking louder than it was five minutes ago.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Thank Webb and head straight to the bridge to brief Wash and Zoe on the timeline. You need your crew informed and prepped for immediate departure—no surprises when the engines fire up.
B: Ask Webb one more critical question: who's the buyer on Ita, and how solid is that connection? If the Alliance is hunting this cargo this hard, you need to know if the endpoint is actually safe.
C: Tell Webb to clear out and disappear. Then gather the crew in the galley for a full briefing—they deserve to know what they're flying with. Transparency now beats betrayal later.
D: Have Webb help you inspect the crate's seals and packaging before he goes. If the Alliance boards, you need to know exactly how obvious this cargo is, and whether it'll pass a cursory look.

data.passage:
You nod toward the crate. "Help me look this over. If they board us, I need to know what they're actually seeing."

Webb's expression shifts—respect flickering across his weathered face. He appreciates a captain who thinks in systems. Together, you kneel beside the polymer container, running your fingers along the sealed edges.

"Good news and bad," Webb murmurs, pointing to the thermal-bonded seams. "These seals are military-grade. Won't show tampering, won't degrade for months. From three feet away, this looks like any other supply shipment."

But when you lean closer, you see them: faint pharmaceutical batch codes stamped into the crate's underside, barely visible unless you know what you're hunting for. Webb traces them with a calloused thumb. "That's the problem. A cursory inspection—manifest check, surface scan—you're clean. But if they actually open this and run diagnostics? Takes maybe five minutes to ID what's inside as controlled substances."

He sits back on his heels. "You've got maybe ten minutes under casual scrutiny before someone notices those codes. After that, you're running."

You absorb this. Ten minutes to convince an Alliance inspector that a shipping manifest is legitimate. Ten minutes before the pharmaceutical markers become undeniable. It's not much, but it's something—a tactical window.

Webb stands, brushing dust from his pants. "I'm gone in the next hour. After that, you're on your own timing."

A.text: Thank Webb and immediately call the crew to the bridge for a full briefing. They deserve to know what they're carrying and what the real timeline is. Unified crew makes better decisions under pressure.

B.text: Ask Webb if there's any way to mask those pharmaceutical codes before departure—a shipping tape overlay, a false label, something that buys you more inspection time. Push him for every trick he knows.

C.text: Keep this inspection between you and Webb for now. Brief only Wash and Zoe on the tactical situation. The fewer people who know the exact seal vulnerability, the fewer people who can accidentally leak it.

D.text: Get Webb's advice on the fastest safe route to Ita that minimizes Alliance patrol exposure. If you're going to run this cargo, you need every advantage—timing, course, and knowledge of where the checkpoints aren't.

PASSAGE:
You nod toward the crate. "Help me look this over. If they board us, I need to know what they're actually seeing."

Webb's expression shifts—respect flickering across his weathered face. He appreciates a captain who thinks in systems. Together, you kneel beside the polymer container, running your fingers along the sealed edges.

"Good news and bad," Webb murmurs, pointing to the thermal-bonded seams. "These seals are military-grade. Won't show tampering, won't degrade for months. From three feet away, this looks like any other supply shipment."

But when you lean closer, you see them: faint pharmaceutical batch codes stamped into the crate's underside, barely visible unless you know what you're hunting for. Webb traces them with a calloused thumb. "That's the problem. A cursory inspection—manifest check, surface scan—you're clean. But if they actually open this and run diagnostics? Takes maybe five minutes to ID what's inside as controlled substances."

He sits back on his heels. "You've got maybe ten minutes under casual scrutiny before someone notices those codes. After that, you're running."

You absorb this. Ten minutes to convince an Alliance inspector that a shipping manifest is legitimate. Ten minutes before the pharmaceutical markers become undeniable. It's not much, but it's something—a tactical window.

Webb stands, brushing dust from his pants. "I'm gone in the next hour. After that, you're on your own timing."

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Thank Webb and immediately call the crew to the bridge for a full briefing. They deserve to know what they're carrying and what the real timeline is. Unified crew makes better decisions under pressure.
B: Ask Webb if there's any way to mask those pharmaceutical codes before departure—a shipping tape overlay, a false label, something that buys you more inspection time. Push him for every trick he knows.
C: Keep this inspection between you and Webb for now. Brief only Wash and Zoe on the tactical situation. The fewer people who know the exact seal vulnerability, the fewer people who can accidentally leak it.
D: Get Webb's advice on the fastest safe route to Ita that minimizes Alliance patrol exposure. If you're going to run this cargo, you need every advantage—timing, course, and knowledge of where the checkpoints aren't.

PASSAGE:
You nod toward the crate. "Help me look this over. If they board us, I need to know what they're actually seeing."

Webb's expression shifts—respect flickering across his weathered face. He appreciates a captain who thinks in systems. Together, you kneel beside the polymer container, running your fingers along the sealed edges.

"Good news and bad," Webb murmurs, pointing to the thermal-bonded seams. "These seals are military-grade. Won't show tampering, won't degrade for months. From three feet away, this looks like any other supply shipment."

But when you lean closer, you see them: faint pharmaceutical batch codes stamped into the crate's underside, barely visible unless you know what you're hunting for. Webb traces them with a calloused thumb. "That's the problem. A cursory inspection—manifest check, surface scan—you're clean. But if they actually open this and run diagnostics? Takes maybe five minutes to ID what's inside as controlled substances."

He sits back on his heels. "You've got maybe ten minutes under casual scrutiny before someone notices those codes. After that, you're running."

You absorb this. Ten minutes to convince an Alliance inspector that a shipping manifest is legitimate. Ten minutes before the pharmaceutical markers become undeniable. It's not much, but it's something—a tactical window.

Webb stands, brushing dust from his pants. "I'm gone in the next hour. After that, you're on your own timing."

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Thank Webb and immediately call the crew to the bridge for a full briefing. They deserve to know what they're carrying and what the real timeline is. Unified crew makes better decisions under pressure.
B: Ask Webb if there's any way to mask those pharmaceutical codes before departure—a shipping tape overlay, a false label, something that buys you more inspection time. Push him for every trick he knows.
C: Keep this inspection between you and Webb for now. Brief only Wash and Zoe on the tactical situation. The fewer people who know the exact seal vulnerability, the fewer people who can accidentally leak it.
D: Get Webb's advice on the fastest safe route to Ita that minimizes Alliance patrol exposure. If you're going to run this cargo, you need every advantage—timing, course, and knowledge of where the checkpoints aren't.

data.passage:
Webb's jaw tightens. For a moment, you think he's going to refuse—that this is where the fixer draws the line. Instead, he exhales slow and reaches into his jacket, producing a roll of thermal-reactive tape, the kind used for high-security cargo manifests. It's worn at the edges, seen some use.

"You're asking the right questions," Webb says, turning it over in his scarred hands. "That's why I'm gonna level with you instead of walking away."

He holds up the tape. "This masks the codes for roughly six hours under standard Alliance scanners. Thermal overlay—they won't see the batch numbers unless they heat-test the crate, which they almost never do on routine inspections." He pauses, meeting your eyes. "Almost."

"But here's what it costs you," Webb continues. "Using this tape? You're not just hiding cargo anymore. You're deliberately tampering with Alliance documentation. That's not a fine—that's conspiracy charges if they catch you. And if they do a heat-test for any reason—temperature variance, equipment malfunction, new protocols—they'll know someone doctored it. They'll know you knew."

Webb places the tape in your palm. It's heavier than it looks.

"Six hours buys you maybe halfway to Ita if Serenity burns hard. After that, the codes show clean again, natural degradation of the overlay. But six hours is also six hours of running hot, six hours closer to Commander Hayes's patrol routes." He steps back. "I'm disappearing in forty minutes. What you do with this is on you, Captain.

A.text: Take the tape and use it immediately. Mask the codes now, undock in the next two hours before Alliance inspectors can get their act together, and run hard for the border. It's a calculated risk, but six hours of cover might be all you need.

B.text: Ask Webb for an alternative—is there a way to move the cargo without using the tape? A different route, a bribe, a contact who can pull strings with the dock authorities? You don't like the idea of deliberately tampering with Alliance documentation.

C.text: Take the tape but don't use it yet. Gather your crew first and tell them the full situation—what's really in the crate, what the Alliance knows, and what using this tape means. Let them vote on whether you're taking this risk together or aborting the job.

D.text: Refuse the tape and demand Webb explain exactly who his buyer on Ita really is and what they plan to do with these pharmaceuticals. If you're going to risk your ship and crew, you need to know whose hands this cargo ends up in and what it's actually for.

PASSAGE:
Webb's jaw tightens. For a moment, you think he's going to refuse—that this is where the fixer draws the line. Instead, he exhales slow and reaches into his jacket, producing a roll of thermal-reactive tape, the kind used for high-security cargo manifests. It's worn at the edges, seen some use.

"You're asking the right questions," Webb says, turning it over in his scarred hands. "That's why I'm gonna level with you instead of walking away."

He holds up the tape. "This masks the codes for roughly six hours under standard Alliance scanners. Thermal overlay—they won't see the batch numbers unless they heat-test the crate, which they almost never do on routine inspections." He pauses, meeting your eyes. "Almost."

"But here's what it costs you," Webb continues. "Using this tape? You're not just hiding cargo anymore. You're deliberately tampering with Alliance documentation. That's not a fine—that's conspiracy charges if they catch you. And if they do a heat-test for any reason—temperature variance, equipment malfunction, new protocols—they'll know someone doctored it. They'll know you knew."

Webb places the tape in your palm. It's heavier than it looks.

"Six hours buys you maybe halfway to Ita if Serenity burns hard. After that, the codes show clean again, natural degradation of the overlay. But six hours is also six hours of running hot, six hours closer to Commander Hayes's patrol routes." He steps back. "I'm disappearing in forty minutes. What you do with this is on you, Captain.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Take the tape and use it immediately. Mask the codes now, undock in the next two hours before Alliance inspectors can get their act together, and run hard for the border. It's a calculated risk, but six hours of cover might be all you need.
B: Ask Webb for an alternative—is there a way to move the cargo without using the tape? A different route, a bribe, a contact who can pull strings with the dock authorities? You don't like the idea of deliberately tampering with Alliance documentation.
C: Take the tape but don't use it yet. Gather your crew first and tell them the full situation—what's really in the crate, what the Alliance knows, and what using this tape means. Let them vote on whether you're taking this risk together or aborting the job.
D: Refuse the tape and demand Webb explain exactly who his buyer on Ita really is and what they plan to do with these pharmaceuticals. If you're going to risk your ship and crew, you need to know whose hands this cargo ends up in and what it's actually for.

PASSAGE:
Webb's jaw tightens. For a moment, you think he's going to refuse—that this is where the fixer draws the line. Instead, he exhales slow and reaches into his jacket, producing a roll of thermal-reactive tape, the kind used for high-security cargo manifests. It's worn at the edges, seen some use.

"You're asking the right questions," Webb says, turning it over in his scarred hands. "That's why I'm gonna level with you instead of walking away."

He holds up the tape. "This masks the codes for roughly six hours under standard Alliance scanners. Thermal overlay—they won't see the batch numbers unless they heat-test the crate, which they almost never do on routine inspections." He pauses, meeting your eyes. "Almost."

"But here's what it costs you," Webb continues. "Using this tape? You're not just hiding cargo anymore. You're deliberately tampering with Alliance documentation. That's not a fine—that's conspiracy charges if they catch you. And if they do a heat-test for any reason—temperature variance, equipment malfunction, new protocols—they'll know someone doctored it. They'll know you knew."

Webb places the tape in your palm. It's heavier than it looks.

"Six hours buys you maybe halfway to Ita if Serenity burns hard. After that, the codes show clean again, natural degradation of the overlay. But six hours is also six hours of running hot, six hours closer to Commander Hayes's patrol routes." He steps back. "I'm disappearing in forty minutes. What you do with this is on you, Captain.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Take the tape and use it immediately. Mask the codes now, undock in the next two hours before Alliance inspectors can get their act together, and run hard for the border. It's a calculated risk, but six hours of cover might be all you need.
B: Ask Webb for an alternative—is there a way to move the cargo without using the tape? A different route, a bribe, a contact who can pull strings with the dock authorities? You don't like the idea of deliberately tampering with Alliance documentation.
C: Take the tape but don't use it yet. Gather your crew first and tell them the full situation—what's really in the crate, what the Alliance knows, and what using this tape means. Let them vote on whether you're taking this risk together or aborting the job.
D: Refuse the tape and demand Webb explain exactly who his buyer on Ita really is and what they plan to do with these pharmaceuticals. If you're going to risk your ship and crew, you need to know whose hands this cargo ends up in and what it's actually for.

C.consequence: Mal takes the tape but defers to crew consensus. Webb approves and vanishes as promised. Make the discussion feel authentic—some crew members angry at being kept in the dark, others understanding the operational security. End with a genuine moment of crew unity, but make clear this is a choice with consequences they're all accepting together.

D.consequence: Mal presses Webb for details about the end-user. Webb's expression hardens—he's already given more than he should. He reveals the buyer is a black-market medical collective on Ita that distributes antivirals to border settlements the Alliance won't serve. The pharmaceuticals are legitimate medicine, not weapons. But Webb also warns: 'The Alliance doesn't care about the medicine. They care about control. If these drugs reach the settlements, people stop depending on Alliance medical stations. That's why they're hunting it.' Webb respects Mal's due diligence but now Mal knows the moral weight—this cargo saves lives, but delivering it is an act of defiance against the Alliance. The tape remains in Mal's hand. Webb disappears in 30 minutes instead of 40.
D.narration_directive: Show Webb's respect for Mal's question, but also his wariness—Mal is asking questions that could get them both killed if overheard. Reveal the cargo's true purpose with weight and gravity. Make clear this is no longer just a smuggling job; it's a choice between Alliance authority and human need. End with Webb's warning about the clock ticking down faster now.
